pending_diagnosis: DIAGNOSIS: Acute Intermittent Porphyria (AIP) THE SMOKING GUN: The combination of acute, disproportionate abdominal pain (a "non-surgical abdomen"), severe autonomic instability (tachycardia, hypertension), profound neuropsychiatric symptoms (psychosis, peripheral neuropathy, seizures, ascending paralysis), refractory hyponatremia (likely SIADH), and the striking darkening of the urine on standing or exposure to light. The final confirmatory test showed massively elevated levels of urinary Porphobilinogen (PBG) and Delta-Aminolevulinic acid (ALA). PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: Acute Intermittent Porphyria is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by a partial deficiency in the enzyme Hydroxymethylbilane Synthase (HMBS), also known as Porphobilinogen Deaminase (PBGD). This deficiency prevents the normal breakdown pathway of heme synthesis. In the latent state, the deficiency is asymptomatic. An acute attack is precipitated by triggers that increase the demand for hepatic heme synthesis (e.g., certain drugs—especially barbiturates, sulfa drugs, alcohol, or progesterone). When triggered, the accumulation of neurotoxic precursors, specifically Porphobilinogen (PBG) and Delta-Aminolevulinic acid (ALA), causes damage. ALA is structurally similar to GABA, disrupting neurotransmission. The resulting toxicity manifests as: 1. Visceral neuropathy (severe abdominal pain). 2. Autonomic neuropathy (tachycardia, labile hypertension). 3. Peripheral neuropathy (motor weakness and ascending paralysis). 4. CNS effects (seizures, psychosis, hyponatremia from inappropriate ADH secretion due to hypothalamic involvement). Treatment focuses on removing the trigger (the anxiolytic, which was phenobarbital-based), high-dose glucose infusion, and prompt administration of intravenous Hematin (heme arginate) to repress ALA synthase activity, thus shutting down the production of the toxic precursors.
